articles.title: Autistic traits in older adults linked to worsening anxiety over time
articles.summary: Aging adults with high autistic traits face a significantly greater risk of worsening anxiety over time, according to new research. The findings highlight a pressing need for targeted mental health support for older individuals on the autism spectrum.
articles.aiSummary: Aging adults with high autistic traits face a significantly greater risk of worsening anxiety over time, according to new research. The findings highlight a pressing need for targeted mental health support for older individuals on the autism spectrum.

articles.title: Personality shifts during adolescence unfold differently for boys and girls
articles.summary: Tracking children from age 10 to 16 shows that both sexes exhibit a decline in conscientiousness and agreeableness. However, researchers found that anxiety and neuroticism tend to increase over this same timeframe exclusively among teenage girls.
articles.aiSummary: Tracking children from age 10 to 16 shows that both sexes exhibit a decline in conscientiousness and agreeableness. However, researchers found that anxiety and neuroticism tend to increase over this same timeframe exclusively among teenage girls.

articles.title: Neural Architecture Search for Generative Adversarial Networks: A Comprehensive Review and Critical Analysis
articles.summary: arXiv:2606.26169v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) has emerged as a pivotal technique in optimizing the design of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), automating the search for effective architectures while addressing the challenges inherent in manual design. This paper provides a comprehensive review of NAS methods applied to GANs, categorizing and comparing various approaches based on criteria such as search strategies, evaluation metrics, and performance outcomes. The review highlights the benefits of NAS in improving GAN performance, stability, and efficien

articles.title: Closing the Loop to Discover Psychological Theories with an Automated Cognitive Scientist
articles.summary: arXiv:2606.26448v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Across the sciences, autonomous systems are increasingly being used in closed-loop discovery, proposing new theories and designing and running experiments to test them. This approach is yet to be applied in the field of cognitive science, where the central bottleneck is theory-building: the creative step of turning the accumulated failures of existing models into better ones. Theory generation has remained manual even as data collection, modeling, and experiment design have been automated. We present the Automated Cognitive Scientist (AutoCog), a

articles.title: Surviving by Serving: Functional Relevance Drives Self-Organization in Complex Adaptive Systems
articles.summary: arXiv:2606.26733v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Complex adaptive systems often develop organized structures without centralized control. Yet the local mechanisms by which functional organization emerges and persists remain incompletely understood. Here we propose Surviving by Serving (SBS) as a general principle of self-organization: components persist as long as their outputs are utilized by other components, whereas prolonged non-utilization promotes adaptation and exploration. To investigate this idea, we introduce a minimal multi-agent model in which agents transform shared resources and r

articles.title: Beyond Single-Source Cognitive Taskonomy:Multi-Source Task Relations through fMRI Transfer Learning
articles.summary: arXiv:2606.26279v1 Announce Type: cross Abstract: Cognitive tasks are organized by shared and specialized neural processes. Masked fMRI reconstruction provides a common self-supervised objective for quantifying transfer relations among task states, but existing reconstruction-based taskonomies mainly study one-to-one transfer from a single source task to a target. Here, we extend an fMRI cognitive taskonomy from single-source to multi-source transfer across 23 Human Connectome Project task states and use Boolean Integer Programming (BIP) to analyze budget-constrained task allocation. We train

articles.title: Power-Law Scaling in the Classification Performance of Small-Scale Spiking Neural Networks
articles.summary: arXiv:2601.14961v2 Announce Type: replace Abstract: This paper investigates the classification capability of small-scale spiking neural networks based on the Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neuron model. We analyze the relationship between classification accuracy and three factors: the number of neurons, the number of stimulus nodes, and the number of classification categories. Notably, we employ a large language model (LLM) to assist in discovering the underlying functional relationships among these variables, and compare its performance against traditional methods such as linear and polynomia

articles.title: Announcing Our Summer Book Club and Podcast
articles.summary: Each summer, we read a novel and explore its themes through conversations with leading thinkers at the intersection of science and culture. This summer, we’ll read Flights by Polish author Olga Tokarczuk, the recipient of the 2018 Nobel Prize in literature. Flights is an enigma of a novel—part memoir, part short story collection, part ethnography of travel—that explores the human impulse toward movement; the perpetual tension between exploring somewhere new or staying put. “Clearly I did not inherit whatever gene it is that makes it so when you linger in a place you start to put down roots,” T

articles.title: Why opposites don’t attract: A global study reveals the true rules of romantic compatibility
articles.summary: A large global study suggests that relationship happiness relies on a specific formula: sharing the same political views, but believing your partner is kinder and more attractive than you are.
articles.aiSummary: A large global study suggests that relationship happiness relies on a specific formula: sharing the same political views, but believing your partner is kinder and more attractive than you are.

articles.title: Brain signals can reveal when a person is preparing to tell a lie
articles.summary: Researchers have found that the brain readies itself to deceive moments before a falsehood is spoken. Tracking this mental preparation could offer a new approach to lie detection.
articles.aiSummary: Researchers have found that the brain readies itself to deceive moments before a falsehood is spoken. Tracking this mental preparation could offer a new approach to lie detection.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Researchers have found that the brain readies itself to deceive moments before a falsehood is spoken. Tracking this mental preparation could offer a new approach to lie detection.

articles.title: An 80-year-old woman with advanced Alzheimer’s regained speech and mobility after taking psilocybin
articles.summary: After years of severe decline, an 80-year-old woman with advanced Alzheimer’s temporarily regained her ability to speak, walk, and interact with family following a massive dose of psilocybin mushrooms, according to a striking new case report.
articles.aiSummary: After years of severe decline, an 80-year-old woman with advanced Alzheimer’s temporarily regained her ability to speak, walk, and interact with family following a massive dose of psilocybin mushrooms, according to a striking new case report.

articles.title: Genetic risk for Alzheimer’s disease could depend on how well you sleep
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered an important link between sleep, genetics, and Alzheimer’s disease. New findings suggest that getting poor sleep can accelerate brain shrinkage and memory loss in older adults carrying specific genetic variants.
articles.aiSummary: Scientists have discovered an important link between sleep, genetics, and Alzheimer’s disease. New findings suggest that getting poor sleep can accelerate brain shrinkage and memory loss in older adults carrying specific genetic variants.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Scientists have discovered an important link between sleep, genetics, and Alzheimer’s disease. New findings suggest that getting poor sleep can accelerate brain shrinkage and memory loss in older adults carrying specific genetic variants.

articles.title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
articles.summary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.
articles.aiSummary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.

articles.title: This common vitamin deficiency can mimic normal aging
articles.summary: Vitamin B12 is needed in microscopic amounts, but a shortage can have major effects on health and energy. The vitamin was first linked to a lifesaving liver treatment for pernicious anemia nearly 100 years ago. Today, researchers are finding that B12 may also help keep cellular powerhouses called mitochondria functioning properly. This could explain why some people experience fatigue and brain fog even before traditional signs of deficiency show up.

articles.title: Brain stimulation technique alters human perception of physical control
articles.summary: Researchers have discovered that applying targeted electrical or magnetic stimulation to a specific brain region can directly alter a person's sense of agency, either improving or impairing their ability to detect subtle outside interference in their physical actions.
articles.aiSummary: Researchers have discovered that applying targeted electrical or magnetic stimulation to a specific brain region can directly alter a person's sense of agency, either improving or impairing their ability to detect subtle outside interference in their physical actions.

articles.title: People who enjoy outshining romantic rivals share distinct psychological traits across cultures
articles.summary: People who love dominating their romantic rivals share a universal psychological profile. A new study spanning three countries links this competitive dating drive to higher aggression, thrill-seeking behavior, and a greater willingness to undergo cosmetic surgery.
articles.aiSummary: People who love dominating their romantic rivals share a universal psychological profile. A new study spanning three countries links this competitive dating drive to higher aggression, thrill-seeking behavior, and a greater willingness to undergo cosmetic surgery.

articles.title: How Can We Feel Loved If We Don’t Feel Known?
articles.summary: For Sonja, researching happiness means being asked over and over again, “What is the secret to happiness?” Sonja has always dreaded that question. It’s ridiculous, it’s reductive, and it’s restrictive. But mostly she has dreaded it because she has never had a solid, science-based answer that she could offer with any confidence. There is no secret to happiness. But there are huge contributors to happiness, and some of them are more powerful than others. Sonja resists being pinned down to just one. But if she were pressed to give an answer, she now has one. She found it when she met Harry, who s

articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…an Airline Pilot
articles.summary: Shooting cat-three landings in the fog, using the “voice of God” on unruly passengers, and declaring Mayday after an engine fire with Paul Drusch, a commercial airline pilot. Why does the pilot’s paycheck start with the parking brake? And what does “sterile cockpit” mean? (Spoiler: it doesn’t mean “clean.”) [buzzsprout episode='19294742' player='true'] View the episode transcript About What It's Like to Be... articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…a Clinical Ethicist
articles.summary: Untangling who gets to speak for a dying patient, weighing a treatment's benefits against its burdens, and searching for clarity in the grayest corners of healthcare with Esther Berkowitz, a clinical ethicist. articles.title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.
